title: US LNG Captures Multi-Year Qatari Supply Outage
catalyst: Qatar's LNG export complex — historically ~20% of global LNG supply — remains effectively offline two months into the Iran conflict, with the Strait of Hormuz still functioning at ~5% of pre-war shipping volume despite a fragile ceasefire. Damaged Qatari trains will take multiple years to fully restore even after hostilities end. Asian buyers, especially Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan, are rotating long-dated supply contracts away from the Gulf and toward US Gulf Coast exporters; Taiwan's CPC just signed a 1.2 MTPA, 24-year offtake with Cheniere running through 2050.
thesis: This is not a temporary price spike — it is a structural reshoring of Asia's LNG supply away from the Persian Gulf. US LNG export capacity is rising from ~17 Bcf/d to ~19 Bcf/d in 2026 with Plaquemines Phase 2 and Golden Pass Train 1, but the demand pull from displaced Qatari volume vastly exceeds new capacity for at least the next 24 months. Long-term Asian SPAs at attractive economics give US LNG operators decades of contracted cash flows, while Henry Hub gas producers feeding the export terminals get a permanent demand floor. The second-order effect — that buyers will pay extra for non-Gulf, non-chokepoint supply — has barely been priced in.

ticker_rationale.LNG: Cheniere is the largest US LNG exporter and the direct counterparty on most of the new Asian SPAs being signed in the wake of the Qatari outage. Corpus Christi Stage 3 is ramping seven new trains, Stage 4 was just filed with FERC (could lift total Corpus Christi capacity to 49 MTPA), and the new 1.2 MTPA CPC Taiwan deal through 2050 illustrates how customers are locking in decades of supply at premium economics. Existing Cheniere capacity is sold out and earning windfall spot margins on uncontracted volumes.
ticker_rationale.EQT: EQT is the largest US natural gas producer with the lowest-cost Appalachian asset base and direct supply relationships to Gulf Coast LNG terminals. Every incremental Bcf/d of US LNG export capacity is essentially a permanent demand sink for Henry Hub gas, and EQT's recent integration of Equitrans midstream gives it firm pipeline capacity into LNG corridors. Higher and more stable Henry Hub pricing flows almost entirely to EQT's free cash flow given its hedging discipline.

risks: A durable Iran ceasefire plus faster-than-expected Qatari repairs could restart Middle East LNG flows by late 2026
Hyperscaler-driven domestic gas demand could crowd out LNG feedgas, narrowing exporter margins
FERC delays or environmental challenges to Stage 4 / next-wave projects could push capacity additions into the 2030s
EQT specifically is exposed to Henry Hub prices, which could decouple from international LNG prices if domestic supply ramps faster than expected
summary: Qatar's LNG export complex (~20% of global supply) remains offline as the Strait of Hormuz crisis drives a structural reshoring of Asian LNG procurement toward US Gulf Coast exporters, with Cheniere signing multi-decade contracts and EQT benefiting from a permanent Henry Hub demand floor.
body_md:
## Setup

Qatar — the world's second-largest LNG supplier and historically responsible for approximately 20% of global LNG supply — halted production after Iranian drones targeted its energy facilities during the Iran conflict that began February 28, 2026 [1]. The Strait of Hormuz, through which virtually all Qatari LNG exports transit, has been effectively closed since the conflict began. Despite a fragile ceasefire, shipping traffic through the Strait remains at approximately 5% of pre-war volume, with Iran's IRGC continuing to fire on commercial vessels and seize container ships [4].

The scale of the disruption is enormous. US producers have been buying natural gas to make LNG at around $3/MMBtu but selling it for approximately $20/MMBtu in Asia and Europe — a margin spread that has not existed since the early days of US LNG exports [1]. US LNG export capacity is rising from approximately 17 Bcf/d at the end of 2025 to slightly more than 19 Bcf/d in 2026 as Corpus Christi Stage 3, Golden Pass Train 1, and Plaquemines Phase 2 ramp up [3]. But the demand pull from displaced Qatari volume vastly exceeds available new capacity for at least the next 24 months.

## Why Now

Three converging dynamics make this more than a temporary commodity trade. First, the physical damage to Qatari LNG infrastructure means that even after hostilities end, full restoration will take years. LNG liquefaction trains are precision-engineered facilities that cannot be quickly repaired — the damaged capacity represents a multi-year supply gap, not a temporary shipping disruption [1].

Second, Asian buyers are structurally reshoring their LNG procurement. Taiwan's CPC Corporation signed a 1.2 MTPA, 24-year offtake agreement with Cheniere running through 2050, building on their existing 2 MTPA deal from 2018 [2]. Japan, South Korea, and other Asian importers are following suit, rotating long-dated supply contracts away from the Gulf and toward US Gulf Coast exporters. This is a permanent shift in global energy trade flows, not a panic reaction — the deals being signed today lock in supply and pricing for decades.

Third, the premium for non-Gulf, non-chokepoint LNG supply has barely been priced into US exporter valuations. Buyers are willing to pay a structural premium for supply that doesn't transit the Strait of Hormuz, the Suez Canal, or other geopolitically exposed chokepoints. This "security of supply" premium represents a new layer of pricing power for US LNG operators that didn't exist before the conflict [6].

## Company-Specific Edge

Cheniere Energy (LNG) is the dominant US LNG exporter and the direct counterparty on most of the new Asian SPAs being signed in the wake of the Qatari outage. The company operates the Sabine Pass and Corpus Christi terminals — the two largest LNG export facilities in the Western Hemisphere. Corpus Christi Stage 3 is currently ramping its final trains (trains 5-7), with all expected online by mid-2026, adding approximately 0.6 Bcf/d of capacity [5]. Stage 4 has been filed with FERC and could lift total Corpus Christi capacity to 49 MTPA [5].

The CPC Taiwan deal illustrates Cheniere's competitive advantage: a 24-year contract indexed to Henry Hub plus a fixed fee provides decades of predictable cash flow with no commodity price risk [2]. Existing Cheniere capacity is fully sold out under long-term contracts, and uncontracted spot volumes are earning windfall margins at current Asian LNG prices. At 12.5x forward P/E with 45% ROE and 26.5% profit margins, Cheniere offers compelling value for a company with multi-decade contracted revenue visibility. IOWN score of 78 (HOLD, nearly BUY).

EQT Corporation (EQT) is the largest US natural gas producer with the lowest-cost asset base in Appalachia. The investment thesis is a second-order derivative: every incremental Bcf/d of US LNG export capacity creates a permanent demand sink for Henry Hub gas, and EQT's recent integration of Equitrans Midstream gives it firm pipeline capacity into the Gulf Coast LNG corridor [1]. Higher and more stable Henry Hub pricing — driven by structural LNG export demand — flows almost entirely to EQT's free cash flow given its hedging discipline and low-cost position.

EQT's Q1 2026 revenue grew 26.9% YoY with EPS growth of 54.6%, and the company trades at just 14.41x forward P/E with 24.93% profit margins. The stock offers both the LNG demand thesis and a domestic gas demand tailwind as hyperscaler data center buildouts create incremental power generation requirements. IOWN score of 62 (HOLD).

## Numbers That Matter

**Cheniere Energy (LNG)**:
- Stock price: $265.58
- Forward P/E: 12.5x
- P/E ratio: 11.0x
- Revenue growth YoY: 5.0%
- Profit margin: 26.5%
- Return on equity: 45.0%
- Debt-to-equity: 220.0%
- Dividend yield: 0.84%
- IOWN overall score: 78 (HOLD)
- CPC Taiwan deal: 1.2 MTPA through 2050
- Stage 3 ramp: trains 5-7 completing mid-2026

**EQT Corporation (EQT)**:
- Stock price: $66.86
- Forward P/E: 14.41x
- P/E ratio: 20.2x
- Revenue growth YoY: 26.9%
- EPS growth YoY: 54.6%
- Profit margin: 24.93%
- Return on equity: 9.01%
- Debt-to-equity: 28.93%
- Dividend yield: 0.99%
- IOWN overall score: 62 (HOLD)

**Market context**:
- Qatar LNG: ~20% of global supply, effectively offline
- US LNG capacity: ~17 Bcf/d rising to ~19 Bcf/d in 2026
- Henry Hub vs. Asian LNG spread: ~$3 vs. ~$20/MMBtu
- Strait of Hormuz: ~5% of pre-war shipping volume

## Bear Case

The most immediate risk is a durable Iran ceasefire and faster-than-expected Qatari infrastructure repairs. If the Strait of Hormuz reopens and Qatar restarts LNG production by late 2026, the supply-shock premium would dissipate and Asian LNG spot prices could fall from $20/MMBtu toward $10-12/MMBtu. The long-term SPAs provide a floor, but spot margins — which are currently driving windfall profitability — would normalize.

Cheniere's debt-to-equity of 220% reflects the capital-intensive nature of LNG infrastructure and creates financial risk if margins compress. FERC delays or environmental challenges to Stage 4 and next-wave projects could push meaningful capacity additions into the 2030s, limiting growth optionality.

EQT faces a different risk: Henry Hub prices could decouple from international LNG prices if domestic supply ramps faster than LNG export demand absorbs it. Hyperscaler-driven domestic gas demand (for data center power generation) could also crowd out LNG feedgas allocation, creating competition for the molecule that narrows exporter margins rather than expanding them.

Broader European energy policy is also shifting. The Bruegel think tank notes that Europe's response to the Iran gas shock should focus on reducing gas dependency through renewable acceleration, not on signing more long-term LNG contracts [6]. If European demand for US LNG plateaus, the growth runway depends entirely on Asian buyers.

## Conclusion

The US LNG thesis is a rare confluence of a geopolitical supply shock (Qatari outage), a structural demand shift (Asian reshoring away from Gulf supply), and confirmed capacity additions (Cheniere Stage 3, Golden Pass). The multi-decade SPAs being signed today lock in profitable economics for US exporters for 20-30 years regardless of how the Iran conflict resolves — the damage to buyer confidence in Gulf supply is already done. Cheniere at 12.5x forward P/E is the primary vehicle; EQT at 14.41x offers the second-order Henry Hub play with lower leverage. Monitor the Hormuz ceasefire progress, quarterly SPA announcements, and FERC permitting timelines as key decision triggers.
invalidation: A durable Iran ceasefire is signed and Qatari LNG production resumes at >50% of pre-war capacity within 6 months
Asian LNG spot prices fall below $12/MMBtu for 30+ consecutive days, signaling the supply-shock premium has dissipated
Cheniere reports declining quarterly LNG export volumes or signals that existing long-term contracts are being renegotiated at lower pricing

metrics_note: 2026-06-18: LNG recommendation upgraded HOLD -> BUY in screener — thesis validated.
